Q1: I tried to set up a routine, but honestly it was pretty chaotic. I'd usually aim to study in the evenings after my extracurricular activities, but with 14 online courses going on, I felt scattered. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend time creating diagrams and color-coded notes, which took longer than just reading. Most weeks I'd get about 8 hours of actual focused study time, which I know wasn't enough given everything I had on my plate.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these bursts of activity right before deadlines, then quieter periods where I'd barely touch certain courses. Being involved in extracurriculars meant some weeks were just impossible to keep consistent. I wanted to be more regular, but life kept getting in the way and I'd have to play catch-up constantly.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or just this anxious feeling that I was falling behind. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because there was something almost addictive about seeing progress bars and earning points. Other times, honestly, I logged in out of guilt more than anything else.
Q4: The gamified elements helped make it more routine for a while. I'd check in to see my progress on the leaderboard or complete quick challenges. For maybe a month, I had this habit of logging in every morning with my coffee. But when my stress levels got really high and I started feeling overwhelmed, that routine fell apart. The habit never fully stuck because my anxiety would make me avoid the platform when things got hard.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm such a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I also found the discussion posts helpful when other students explained concepts differently than the instructor. The gamified tasks made boring content more bearable and gave me small wins when everything else felt like I was drowning.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials, especially the dense PDFs. They just didn't work for how my brain processes information. I'd skim them at best or skip them entirely if there was a video covering the same topic. Some of the optional practice exercises I kept putting off because they didn't count toward my grade and I was already stretched so thin.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate progress or if it directly connected to what would be on the exam. The gamified tasks were great because they gave instant feedback and made me feel like I was accomplishing something. If something felt abstract or disconnected from my goals, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to see the point, especially when I was this stressed.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're juggling 14 courses and your anxiety is through the roof, you triage everything. Required activities got done—sometimes barely, sometimes late—but they got done. Optional stuff felt like it was designed for students with more time and less on their minds. I'd feel guilty skipping them but not guilty enough to actually do them.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded tons of materials and studied them on my tablet during commutes or while waiting between activities. I made handwritten visual notes and mind maps that I'd review constantly. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes and diagrams, working through problems on paper, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also study with classmates in person, quizzing each other and explaining concepts. Sometimes I'd watch the videos once, download the slides, and then spend hours working with those offline. The LMS only saw the tip of the iceberg.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the platform, maybe twice if it was complex, then download any available materials and work with them offline. My internet isn't always reliable, so having offline access was essential. I'd return to those downloaded PDFs and slides repeatedly, but the system would only record that initial access.
Q12: Around midterms, everything crashed for me emotionally. My anxiety spiked, I felt bored with content I didn't understand, and the workload across all my courses became impossible. There were days I'd stare at the login screen and just couldn't bring myself to click. The stress made everything feel pointless, even though I knew I needed to push through. Those were dark weeks.
Q13: Massively. When I was exhausted from extracurriculars or overwhelmed by competing deadlines, the LMS became something I dreaded. I'd log in, feel the weight of everything I hadn't done, and sometimes just log out without doing anything productive. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely. My mental state directly controlled how much I could engage.
Q14: Yes, more times than I want to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other assignments or just zoning out from exhaustion. I'd scroll through materials without really reading them, just going through the motions. It looked like engagement on paper, but I wasn't actually absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like wasted time.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible—like if I didn't start, I couldn't fail at it. I knew this was irrational but knowing that didn't help. Some tasks felt so overwhelming that avoidance became my coping mechanism. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my only motivation. I'd cram everything into the last possible hours, which explains my inconsistent assignment completion. The panic of an approaching deadline would finally override my avoidance. But this meant I was always rushing, never doing my best work. The gamified tasks with shorter deadlines actually helped break this pattern sometimes.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going. But negative feedback or poor grades would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid that course for days. I needed encouragement, not just criticism. The delayed feedback on some assignments meant I'd already moved on mentally before learning what I did wrong. Faster, more supportive feedback would have helped my anxiety.
Q18: The course structure was overwhelming with so much content spread across the semester. I appreciated when modules were clearly organized visually, but some sections felt like mazes. The gamified elements added structure that helped me pace myself better. When the structure was unclear, my anxiety made it ten times worse because I'd worry I was missing something important.
Q19: Visual organization made engagement easier—clear module layouts, progress indicators, color-coding. The gamification definitely helped make things feel more manageable and even fun sometimes. What made it harder was when there were too many clicks to find things, or when important information was buried in text-heavy pages. My brain just couldn't process walls of text, especially when stressed.
Q20: That was literally me. I'd log in frequently, watch videos, click around, participate in discussions—but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The anxiety around being judged on assignments was paralyzing. It's easier to passively consume content than to put yourself out there with submitted work. High activity doesn't mean you've conquered the fear of failure.
Q21: I think some students are just more efficient or better at offline studying. If you're good at absorbing information quickly and can perform on exams without constant LMS interaction, low activity doesn't mean low learning. I wish that were me, but my exam scores show it wasn't. Still, I understand how someone could learn effectively without the platform showing it.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they completely miss the quality of that time and what's happening in your head. They can't see anxiety, boredom, or genuine understanding. They miss offline work entirely. A log might show I watched a video for 60 minutes but can't tell if I understood it or was crying from stress halfway through.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it. It's when your brain is actively working on the material, not just your eyes scanning it. For me, engagement also means feeling like I can succeed, which my anxiety often blocked. True engagement is emotional and intellectual investment, not just showing up and clicking buttons.
Q24: The LMS would never show how hard I was fighting just to keep going. It wouldn't show the panic attacks before logging in, or the hours spent with handwritten notes, or the times I helped classmates understand things even when I was struggling myself. It can't capture resilience—how I kept coming back despite failing, despite the anxiety, despite feeling bored and overwhelmed. That fight doesn't leave a data trail.
